title: First tummy time
summary: Short, supervised tummy time builds neck and shoulder strength.
steps: Lay your baby tummy-down on a firm, flat surface or across your lap.
Get down to their level and talk or sing to encourage them to lift their head.
Start with 1–2 minutes, a few times a day, and build up gradually.
Always stay with them and stop if they get upset.
whyItHelps: Strengthens the muscles babies need to lift their head, roll, and eventually sit.

title: Face-to-face gazing
summary: Hold your face close and let your baby study you.
steps: Hold your baby about 8–12 inches from your face during a calm, alert moment.
Make eye contact, smile, and slowly change your expressions.
Talk softly and pause to give them a turn to respond.
whyItHelps: Newborns see best up close; faces are their favorite way to learn about the world and bond.

title: High-contrast viewing
summary: Show bold black-and-white patterns to engage developing vision.
steps: Hold a high-contrast card or book about 8–12 inches away.
Slowly move it side to side and watch if their eyes follow.
Pause on patterns they seem to like.
whyItHelps: Bold contrast is easiest for newborn vision to focus on and encourages visual tracking.

title: Talk and sing through the day
summary: Narrate everyday moments to bathe your baby in language.
steps: Describe what you're doing as you change, feed, or carry them — in your mother tongue.
Use a warm, sing-song voice and exaggerated expressions; a lori (lullaby) works beautifully.
Pause often so they can coo or react back.
whyItHelps: Hearing lots of warm, responsive speech builds the foundation for language. A multilingual home is an asset, not a confusion.

title: Smile and respond
summary: Reward your baby's smiles and coos with your own.
steps: When your baby smiles or coos, smile and coo back.
Leave a pause for them to take another turn.
Keep the back-and-forth going as long as they're enjoying it.
whyItHelps: These 'serve and return' exchanges build social skills and secure attachment.

title: Follow the rattle
summary: Move a rattle slowly so your baby tracks it with their eyes.
steps: Hold a rattle about a foot from your baby's face.
Shake it gently, then move it slowly side to side and up and down.
Encourage them to follow and, later, to swipe at it.
whyItHelps: Builds visual tracking and links sight, sound, and reaching.

title: Mirror tummy time
summary: An unbreakable mirror makes tummy time more inviting.
steps: Place a baby-safe mirror upright in front of your baby during tummy time.
Point to the 'baby' in the mirror and talk about it.
Celebrate when they lift their head to look.
whyItHelps: Motivates head control and introduces early self-discovery.

title: Reach for the toy
summary: Offer a toy just within reach to invite grasping.
steps: Hold a lightweight toy a little in front of your baby.
Let them swipe, then bring it close enough to grab.
Let them explore it with hands and mouth.
whyItHelps: Develops reaching, grasping, and hand–eye coordination.

title: Bicycle legs
summary: Gently move your baby's legs in a cycling motion.
steps: Lay your baby on their back during a happy, alert time.
Hold their lower legs and gently move them in slow bicycle circles.
Talk or sing along with the rhythm.
whyItHelps: Builds body awareness and can ease gas and fussiness.

title: Read together
summary: Share simple cloth or board books every day.
steps: Snuggle up and hold a sturdy cloth or board book where your baby can see it.
Point to pictures and name them in your mother tongue; use voices and sounds.
Let your baby touch, pat, and (gently) mouth the book.
Follow their lead — it's fine to skip pages or stop early.
whyItHelps: Daily reading, even in any language you speak at home, grows vocabulary, attention, and a love of books.

title: Texture exploration
summary: Offer safe household objects with different textures to touch.
steps: Gather safe items from around the house: a smooth steel katori, a wooden spoon, soft cotton cloth, a silk dupatta.
Let your baby touch and hold each one while you name the texture.
Watch closely and keep small or loose parts away.
whyItHelps: Sensory exploration builds curiosity and fine-motor awareness.

title: Roll practice
summary: Encourage rolling with a tempting toy to the side.
steps: Lay your baby on their back on a soft, safe surface.
Hold a favorite toy out to one side to encourage them to turn.
Give a gentle, supportive hand if they're nearly there.
Cheer every attempt.
whyItHelps: Practices the coordination and strength needed to roll over.

title: Peekaboo
summary: Hide your face and reappear with a smile.
steps: Cover your face with your hands or a light cloth.
Pause, then reveal your face with a cheerful 'peekaboo!'
Repeat and watch their anticipation grow.
whyItHelps: Introduces object permanence and delights their social side.

title: Supported sitting play
summary: Practice sitting with toys arranged in a circle.
steps: Sit your baby up, using a cushion or your body for support.
Place a few toys within easy reach around them.
Let them reach and lean to build balance; stay close to catch them.
whyItHelps: Strengthens core muscles and balance for independent sitting.

title: Pass it along
summary: Encourage moving a toy from one hand to the other.
steps: Hand your baby a light, easy-to-grip toy.
Offer a second toy to the same hand so they pass the first across.
Name what they're doing as they explore.
whyItHelps: Builds hand-to-hand transfer and coordination between both sides of the body.

title: Mirror play
summary: Explore faces and expressions together in a mirror.
steps: Sit together in front of a safe mirror.
Point to features: 'There's your nose! There's mine!'
Make expressions and sounds and watch their reaction.
whyItHelps: Supports self-awareness, social engagement, and body vocabulary.

title: Where did it go?
summary: Hide a toy under a cloth and let your baby find it.
steps: Show your baby a favorite toy, then cover it with a light cloth while they watch.
Ask 'Where did it go?' and encourage them to uncover it.
Celebrate when they find it, then play again.
whyItHelps: Reinforces object permanence and problem-solving.

title: Crawling course
summary: Set up cushions and toys to motivate moving.
steps: Place a favorite toy just out of reach to encourage crawling toward it.
Add soft cushions or tunnels for safe climbing and crawling over.
Stay close and cheer their efforts.
whyItHelps: Builds strength, coordination, and confidence in moving independently.

title: Name it
summary: Label objects, people, and actions all day long.
steps: Point to and name everyday things: 'cup', 'dog', 'ball'.
Pause so your baby can babble back, then repeat the word.
Connect words to actions: 'up!', 'all done', 'bye-bye'.
whyItHelps: Repeated, in-context words build the vocabulary behind first words.

title: Stack and knock down
summary: Build a small tower and let your baby topple it.
steps: Stack two or three soft blocks or nesting steel katoris.
Invite your baby to knock them over — half the fun!
Hand them a block or katori to try stacking one themselves.
whyItHelps: Develops hand control, cause-and-effect, and early problem-solving.

title: Cruising encouragement
summary: Set up sturdy supports for side-stepping practice.
steps: Arrange stable furniture close together at your baby's chest height.
Place toys along the way to encourage stepping sideways.
Stay alongside to steady them and prevent falls.
whyItHelps: Builds the balance and leg strength that lead to walking.

title: In and out
summary: Fill and empty a container with safe objects.
steps: Give your baby a steel tumbler or katori and a few safe, chunky objects.
Show them how to drop objects in and tip them out.
Narrate 'in… and out!' as they play.
whyItHelps: Teaches spatial concepts, fine-motor control, and cause-and-effect.

title: Pat-a-cake and gestures
summary: Play clapping and waving games together.
steps: Sing a clapping rhyme — 'taali bajao' or pat-a-cake — and guide your baby's hands to clap.
Practice waving 'ta-ta'/bye-bye and folding hands for namaste at natural moments.
Pause to let them try the gesture themselves.
whyItHelps: Pairs language with gesture and builds joyful social turn-taking.

title: Point and name
summary: Follow your baby's pointing and supply the words.
steps: When your baby points or looks at something, name it for them.
Add a short comment: 'That's a dog. The dog says woof!'
Encourage them to point and 'ask' for things.
whyItHelps: Shared attention and naming are powerful drivers of first words.

title: Daily oil malish
summary: A gentle oil massage before bath — a calming Indian routine that supports body awareness and bonding.
steps: Warm a little baby-safe oil between your palms in a warm, draught-free room.
With light, slow strokes, massage the legs, arms, back, and tummy — never pressing on the soft spot or spine hard.
Talk or sing softly and watch their cues; stop if they fuss.
Keep it short for newborns and follow with a warm bath.
whyItHelps: Loving touch soothes baby, supports body awareness and circulation, and is a treasured bonding routine in Indian homes.

title: Floor play on a mat
summary: Lots of free time on a firm mat or dhurrie is the best gym for rolling, sitting, and crawling.
steps: Spread a clean, firm mat or dhurrie on the floor in a safe, open space.
Lay baby down with a few toys just within reach to tempt rolling and reaching.
Let them move freely — skip walkers and propped seats, which slow motor practice.
Stay close and join them at their level.
whyItHelps: Open floor time on a firm surface gives babies the freedom to practise rolling, sitting, and crawling on their own timeline.

title: Self-feeding finger foods
summary: Let baby pick up soft finger foods themselves — messy, but great for the pincer grasp.
steps: Once baby sits well, offer soft finger foods: small pieces of idli, soft roti, ripe banana, or steamed carrot.
Sit with them and let them grasp and bring food to their mouth — expect mess!
Offer water in a small katori or cup.
Always supervise closely; avoid hard, round, or whole nuts (choking risk).
whyItHelps: Self-feeding builds the pincer grasp, hand-to-mouth coordination, and a happy, independent relationship with food.
